component_identification.content: Rulebook contents list: 1 game board with scoring track, 84 illustrated cards, 36 voting tokens in 6 colors (numbered 1–6), and 6 wooden rabbit pawns. Setup text confirms each player receives 6 image cards, places their rabbit at 0, and takes voting tokens matching current player count (1–4 with 4 players, 1–5 with 5 players, 1–6 with 6 players). No hidden extras are required beyond the draw pile made from undealt cards. For table checks at a café: verify card deck is complete at 84, each player color has a rabbit, and token numbers are not mixed between sets. If running 3-player variant from the rules, players use 7 cards each and non-storytellers submit 2 cards each round; this changes card flow but not component identity.

core_game_loop.content: Each round one player is the storyteller. The storyteller picks one card from hand and says a clue aloud without showing the card (word, phrase, sentence, sound, quotation, etc.). Every other player chooses one card from their hand that could match the clue and passes it face down to the storyteller, who shuffles all submitted cards with their own and lays them out in numbered positions. Non-storytellers secretly vote on which displayed card they believe is the storyteller’s card, then all votes are revealed and scored. Players draw back to a full hand and storyteller passes left clockwise.

detailed_rules.content: Setup from the rulebook is strict: choose colors, place rabbits on 0, shuffle all 84 cards, deal 6 to each player, and create a face-down draw pile with the rest. Hands stay hidden. The storyteller phase is intentionally open-ended: clues can be literal or associative, spoken text or other performative clue forms depending on variant. After hearing the clue, non-storytellers choose exactly one matching card from hand (two each in the 3-player variant) and pass it face down. The storyteller randomizes all submissions, then reveals them as a numbered line from left to right. Voting is simultaneous and secret using numbered tokens corresponding to card positions; storyteller never votes and a player cannot vote for their own submitted card. After reveal, place votes on the chosen cards before calculating points. Scoring logic from the rulebook: if everybody finds the storyteller card, or nobody finds it, storyteller scores 0 while every other player scores 2. Otherwise storyteller scores 3 and every player who voted correctly scores 3. In addition, every non-storyteller gains +1 for each vote placed on their submitted decoy card. Move pawns on track accordingly. End-of-turn cleanup: discard used cards, refill hands to 6, pass storyteller role clockwise. The rules’ tips emphasize balance: clues that are too obvious or too abstract both punish storyteller scoring. Variant text additionally permits singing/miming clue styles, and groups may combine variants by agreement.

scoring_and_endgame.content: All scoring comes from round voting outcomes. Storyteller points: either 0 (if all or none guessed correctly) or 3 (if some but not all guessed correctly). Guesser points: 3 for correctly identifying storyteller card when that middle condition is met; otherwise non-storytellers get 2 each in all/none cases. Decoy bonus: each non-storyteller receives 1 point per vote attracted to their submitted card. End condition per rulebook: the game ends when the last card from the deck has been drawn (after completing the round sequence). Winner is the player furthest ahead on scoring track; tied leaders share victory unless house tiebreak is announced beforehand.

beginner_strategy.content: As storyteller, aim for a clue with medium precision: you want some people to identify your card, not everyone and not nobody. Use references your table can plausibly recognize; highly private references reduce guessability too far. As a guesser, evaluate the storyteller’s style history—are they usually poetic, literal, pop-culture, or emotional? Submit decoy cards that are believable alternatives, because vote-attraction points are a major scoring source. Track hand composition: burning your strongest “universal” card too early can leave weak options when you become storyteller. In 3-player mode (two-card submissions), expect denser board ambiguity and adjust clue tightness slightly upward.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint using official rulebook text from dixit.txt as primary source. Reference snippet: - 36 voting tokens in 6 different colours numbered from 1 to 6
